summary:
The abolition of the transatlantic slave trade was one of the great moral and political transformations of the nineteenth century, achieved through a combination of ideological campaigning, legislative action, diplomatic pressure, and naval enforcement. Britain's Slave Trade Act of 1807 prohibited British subjects from participating in the trade, and the subsequent decades saw Britain use its naval power and diplomatic influence to pressure other nations to follow suit — with mixed and often slow results.

The suppression effort was a complex international project. The Royal Navy's West Africa Squadron patrolled Atlantic shipping lanes from 1808 onward, intercepting slave ships and liberating their captives to the colony of Sierra Leone. By mid-century the squadron was deploying some 25 vessels and freeing thousands of enslaved people annually, yet the trade proved resilient: demand from Cuba and Brazil sustained trafficking well into the 1860s, and suppression operations faced legal and diplomatic obstacles from nations unwilling to grant Britain search-and-seizure rights over their ships.

Legal abolition of slavery itself — distinct from the trade — proceeded unevenly. Britain emancipated enslaved people in its colonies in 1833–38; France in 1848 (for the second time, having briefly emancipated and then re-enslaved); the United States in 1865 following the Civil War; Brazil in 1888, the last country in the Americas to do so. The persistence of domestic slavery in Brazil and Cuba, sustained by illegal imports, meant that the transatlantic trade continued clandestinely for decades after its formal prohibition, finally ending only when the major receiving markets themselves abolished the institution.
